title: Somatic Experiencing vs EMDR: Which Is Right for You?
excerpt: Explore the key differences between Somatic Experiencing and EMDR to find the best trauma healing approach for your nervous system.
body:
Understanding Trauma and Its Impact on the Body

Trauma isn’t just a mental or emotional event; it’s a full-body experience that imprints itself on the nervous system. As Bessel van der Kolk explains in The Body Keeps the Score, trauma disrupts our physiological regulation, often leading to stuck energy, hypervigilance, or numbness. For many who’ve tried traditional talk therapy but still feel stuck, body-centered approaches like Somatic Experiencing (SE) and Eye Movement Desensitization and Reprocessing (EMDR) offer promising pathways to healing.

Before choosing a modality, it’s essential to understand how each works, what their strengths are, and how they engage your body and nervous system differently.

What Is Somatic Experiencing?

Developed by Peter Levine, Somatic Experiencing is a trauma healing method grounded in the idea that trauma resides in the body rather than the narrative alone. Levine’s work draws on observations of animals in the wild, who naturally discharge energy after a threat without developing trauma symptoms. SE helps clients access and release the trapped energy and incomplete defensive responses stored in the nervous system.

Rather than focusing on the story of trauma, SE emphasizes sensation awareness and incremental processing of physical felt experience. Clients are guided to notice subtle body sensations, shifts in tension, and changes in breathing. The therapist helps titrate exposure to traumatic material to avoid overwhelm, facilitating a gradual restoration of nervous system regulation.

“Trauma is not what happens to you, but what happens inside you as a result of what happened to you.” – Peter Levine

How SE Works in Practice

  • Tracking Sensations: The client learns to tune into their body’s sensations (tingling, warmth, tension, movement impulses).
  • Titration: Exposure to traumatic material is carefully paced to prevent flooding the nervous system.
  • Pendulation: Moving between states of arousal and calm to restore balance.
  • Discharge: Allowing natural release of energy through shaking, yawning, or deep breaths.

What Is EMDR?

EMDR, developed by Francine Shapiro, is a structured therapy that aims to reprocess traumatic memories through bilateral stimulation—typically eye movements, taps, or sounds. EMDR is grounded in the Adaptive Information Processing model, positing that trauma memories are improperly stored and isolated in the brain. Bilateral stimulation is thought to unlock and integrate these memories into adaptive networks.

EMDR sessions involve recalling a traumatic event while simultaneously engaging in bilateral stimulation. This process reduces the emotional charge and negative beliefs associated with the trauma, helping clients develop healthier perspectives and emotional responses.

How EMDR Works in Practice

  • Assessment: Identifying target memories, negative beliefs, and desired positive beliefs.
  • Desensitization: Focusing on the memory while performing bilateral stimulation.
  • Installation: Strengthening positive beliefs related to the memory.
  • Body Scan: Checking for residual tension or distress in the body.
  • Closure: Returning to a calm state before ending the session.

Key Differences Between Somatic Experiencing and EMDR

While both SE and EMDR are effective for trauma recovery, their approaches and emphases differ significantly:

  • Focus: SE centers on bodily sensations and nervous system regulation; EMDR focuses on reprocessing traumatic memories with bilateral stimulation.
  • Process: SE uses gradual, felt sensing and discharge; EMDR uses structured phases targeting specific memories.
  • Nervous System Emphasis: SE explicitly targets autonomic nervous system dysregulation; EMDR addresses this indirectly through memory processing.
  • Client Experience: SE is often slower-paced and nonverbal; EMDR involves verbal processing alongside bilateral stimulation.
  • Research Base: EMDR has extensive empirical support for PTSD; SE is supported by neurobiological theory and growing clinical evidence.

Who Might Benefit Most from Somatic Experiencing?

Somatic Experiencing is especially helpful for people who:

  • Feel stuck in their bodies with chronic tension, dissociation, or hyperarousal.
  • Have difficulty verbalizing trauma or accessing memories.
  • Experience somatic symptoms like chronic pain, digestive issues, or unexplained fatigue.
  • Want a gradual, body-centered approach that prioritizes nervous system regulation.

As Peter Levine and Pat Ogden emphasize, SE’s focus on restoring autonomic balance addresses trauma’s root in the body’s survival system. For those with complex trauma, developmental trauma, or somatic symptoms, SE can provide a foundational sense of safety and embodiment.

Who Might Benefit Most from EMDR?

EMDR is often recommended for individuals who:

  • Have identifiable traumatic memories or PTSD symptoms.
  • Want a structured, time-limited therapy with clear phases.
  • Are comfortable engaging with trauma narratives and cognitive reframing.
  • May benefit from rapid symptom reduction through bilateral stimulation.

Francine Shapiro’s work has demonstrated EMDR’s efficacy for combat veterans, abuse survivors, and others with post-traumatic stress disorder. EMDR’s cognitive and memory-based approach suits people ready to process trauma stories and reframe negative beliefs.

Scientific Perspectives on Nervous System Regulation

Stephen Porges’ Polyvagal Theory offers a useful framework for understanding both modalities. The theory highlights the role of the autonomic nervous system in trauma responses—specifically the balance between the sympathetic (fight/flight) and parasympathetic (freeze/shutdown) systems.

Somatic Experiencing directly engages this system by helping clients renegotiate defensive responses and restore ventral vagal regulation. EMDR, while less explicitly focused on the nervous system, appears to facilitate neural integration and calming of hyperarousal through bilateral stimulation.

Deb Dana, a leading clinician applying Polyvagal Theory, notes that therapies which support autonomic flexibility and safety promote lasting healing. Both SE and EMDR can contribute to this, but SE’s somatic focus often makes autonomic shifts more accessible.

Integrating Somatic Experiencing and EMDR

It’s important to recognize these approaches aren’t mutually exclusive. Many trauma therapists integrate somatic awareness and nervous system regulation strategies with EMDR’s reprocessing phases. As Dan Siegel and Richard Schwartz advocate through their integration models, healing benefits from addressing multiple levels—body, mind, and memory.

Clients may begin with SE to establish safety and calm nervous system reactivity, then transition to EMDR to process specific traumatic memories. Alternatively, EMDR sessions might incorporate somatic check-ins and grounding techniques to enhance safety.

Making Your Choice: Factors to Consider

  • Your Relationship to Your Body: If you feel disconnected or overwhelmed by somatic sensations, SE’s gentle tracking may help you build that connection safely.
  • Readiness to Engage Trauma Memories: EMDR requires revisiting trauma narratives, which can feel intense. If you prefer a less cognitive approach, SE might be better initially.
  • Therapist Expertise and Approach: Seek a practitioner trained in your preferred modality who respects your pace and nervous system signals.
  • Symptom Profile: If you experience flashbacks, nightmares, and PTSD symptoms, EMDR’s evidence base is strong. For chronic somatic complaints and autonomic dysregulation, SE may be more appropriate.

Conclusion

Somatic Experiencing and EMDR are both powerful trauma healing tools grounded in real science and clinical experience. Your choice depends on your unique needs, symptom patterns, and comfort with body or memory-focused work.

As Gabor Maté emphasizes, trauma healing requires compassion, patience, and attunement to the nervous system’s wisdom. Whether you choose SE, EMDR, or a blend of both, prioritizing your body’s signals and pacing yourself will foster the deep nervous system regulation that leads to lasting recovery.

Consult with a trauma-informed clinician to explore which modality aligns best with your healing journey.

“Healing is a matter of time, but it is sometimes also a matter of opportunity.” – Hippocrates

faqs.q: Can Somatic Experiencing and EMDR be used together?
faqs.a: Yes, many therapists integrate both approaches. SE can help establish bodily safety and regulation before EMDR’s memory processing phases.

faqs.q: Which therapy is faster for trauma recovery?
faqs.a: EMDR often leads to faster symptom reduction, especially for PTSD. SE tends to be slower but builds deep nervous system resilience.

faqs.q: Is Somatic Experiencing appropriate for all trauma types?
faqs.a: SE is effective for many trauma types, especially complex or developmental trauma, but it’s important to work with a trained practitioner to tailor treatment.

faqs.q: Does EMDR require recalling details of trauma?
faqs.a: Yes, EMDR involves engaging with traumatic memories, but the bilateral stimulation helps reduce distress during processing.
